--- title: "[Parametric]{.doc-object-name .doc-class .doc-label .doc-label-class}" body-classes: doc-api-page page-navigation: false html-table-processing: none --- ::: {.doc-subject} Univariate parametric survival distribution. ::: ::: {.doc-usage-source} Usage [Source](https://github.com/rich-iannone/greenwood/blob/main/greenwood/_univariate.py#L57-L600){target="_blank" rel="noopener"} ::: ::: {.doc-signature .doc-Kind.CLASS} ```python Parametric(dist="weibull", *, conf_level=0.95) ``` ::: ::: {.doc-text} Fits a parametric survival distribution to right-censored data by maximum likelihood, without covariates. The result is a fully specified distribution that provides survival, hazard, density, quantile, and mean-life predictions. Use this when you want to explore which distributional family best describes your data (Weibull vs. log-normal vs. log-logistic) before moving to regression modelling, or when the scientific question is simply "what is the median survival time and its uncertainty?" ::: ## Parameters {.doc-parameters} ::: {.doc-definition-items} [dist]{.doc-parameter-name}[:]{.doc-parameter-annotation-sep} [str]{.doc-parameter-annotation} [=]{.doc-parameter-default-sep .op} [["weibull"]{.st}]{.doc-parameter-default} : Distribution family: `"weibull"` (default), `"exponential"`, `"lognormal"`, or `"loglogistic"`. [conf_level]{.doc-parameter-name}[:]{.doc-parameter-annotation-sep} [float]{.doc-parameter-annotation} [=]{.doc-parameter-default-sep .op} [[0.95]{.fl}]{.doc-parameter-default} : Confidence level for parameter intervals (default `0.95`). ::: ## Returns {.doc-returns} ::: {.doc-definition-items} [Parametric]{.doc-parameter-annotation} : The fitted estimator (after calling `fit()`), with attributes `params_`, `std_error_`, `conf_low_`, `conf_high_`, `loglik_`, `aic_`, `bic_`, `n_`, and `n_event_`. ::: ## Details {.doc-details} Internally every family is fitted in the AFT location–scale parameterisation ($\log T = \mu + \sigma\varepsilon$), and results are reported back in the natural parameterisation of each distribution (e.g., Weibull shape and scale). Standard errors are obtained via the delta method applied to the observed information matrix. Model selection between families is straightforward: lower AIC (or BIC) indicates a better fit. Use `compare_distributions()` to rank all four families at once. ## Examples {.doc-examples} Fit a Weibull distribution to the lung cancer dataset and inspect the parameter estimates: ::: {#5e81f77e .cell execution_count=1} ``` {.python .cell-code} import greenwood as gw lung = gw.load_dataset("lung", backend="polars") y = gw.Surv.right(lung["time"], event=(lung["status"] == 2)) fit = gw.Parametric("weibull").fit(y) fit ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-display execution_count=1} ``` Parametric (weibull distribution) estimate std_error shape 1.317 0.08221 scale 417.8 24.7 n = 228, events = 165 Log-likelihood = -1154 AIC = 2312, BIC = 2319 ``` ::: ::: To quickly compare all four distributions and pick the best-fitting family, use the companion function `compare_distributions()`: ::: {#d273e6fa .cell execution_count=2} ``` {.python .cell-code} gw.compare_distributions(y, format="polars") ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-display execution_count=2} ```{=html}
shape: (4, 5)
distn_paramsloglikaicbic
stri64f64f64f64
"weibull"2-1153.8511882311.7023762318.561067
"loglogistic"2-1160.9306242325.8612472332.719938
"exponential"1-1162.3381762326.6763522330.105697
"lognormal"2-1169.2690552342.5381112349.396802
``` ::: ::: ## Methods {.doc-methods}
Name Description
cumulative_hazard() Cumulative hazard function $H(t) = -\log S(t)$ at the given times.
density() Probability density function $f(t)$ at the given times.
fit() Fit the distribution to right-censored survival data by maximum likelihood.
hazard() Hazard function $h(t) = f(t) / S(t)$ at the given times.
mean() Expected survival time $E[T]$.
median() Median survival time (the 50th-percentile time).
quantile() Quantile function: survival-time quantiles at failure probabilities `p`.
survival() Survival function $S(t) = P(T > t)$ at the given times.
to_frame() Return the parameter estimates as a tidy DataFrame.
### [cumulative_hazard()]{.doc-object-name .doc-function .doc-label .doc-label-method} ::: {.doc-subject} Cumulative hazard function $H(t) = -\log S(t)$ at the given times. ::: ::: {.doc-usage-source} Usage [Source](https://github.com/rich-iannone/greenwood/blob/main/greenwood/_univariate.py#L327-L362){target="_blank" rel="noopener"} ::: ::: {.doc-signature .doc-Kind.FUNCTION} ```python cumulative_hazard(times) ``` ::: ::: {.doc-text} The cumulative hazard summarises the total accumulated risk up to time $t$. It increases monotonically from zero and is unbounded. A steeper rise indicates higher event rates over that interval. ::: #### Parameters {.doc-parameters} ::: {.doc-definition-items} [times]{.doc-parameter-name}[:]{.doc-parameter-annotation-sep} [Any]{.doc-parameter-annotation} : Query times (array-like of positive floats). ::: #### Returns {.doc-returns} ::: {.doc-definition-items} [ndarray]{.doc-parameter-annotation} : Cumulative hazard values, same length as `times`. ::: #### Details {.doc-details} Computed as the negative log of the survival function: $H(t) = -\log S(t)$. For a Weibull distribution this simplifies to $H(t) = (t/\lambda)^k$. #### Examples {.doc-examples} Compute the cumulative hazard at several time points under a fitted Weibull model: ::: {#2d103f26 .cell execution_count=3} ``` {.python .cell-code} import greenwood as gw lung = gw.load_dataset("lung", backend="polars") y = gw.Surv.right(lung["time"], event=(lung["status"] == 2)) fit = gw.Parametric("weibull").fit(y) fit.cumulative_hazard([100, 200, 365, 500]) ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-display execution_count=3} ``` array([0.15217398, 0.37909564, 0.83712486, 1.2669871 ]) ``` ::: ::: ### [density()]{.doc-object-name .doc-function .doc-label .doc-label-method} ::: {.doc-subject} Probability density function $f(t)$ at the given times. ::: ::: {.doc-usage-source} Usage [Source](https://github.com/rich-iannone/greenwood/blob/main/greenwood/_univariate.py#L411-L452){target="_blank" rel="noopener"} ::: ::: {.doc-signature .doc-Kind.FUNCTION} ```python density(times) ``` ::: ::: {.doc-text} Returns the density of the survival-time distribution. The density is the derivative of the CDF, so $f(t)\,dt$ gives the probability of the event occurring in a small interval around $t$. Useful for plotting the fitted distribution against a histogram of observed times. ::: #### Parameters {.doc-parameters} ::: {.doc-definition-items} [times]{.doc-parameter-name}[:]{.doc-parameter-annotation-sep} [Any]{.doc-parameter-annotation} : Query times (array-like of positive floats). ::: #### Returns {.doc-returns} ::: {.doc-definition-items} [ndarray]{.doc-parameter-annotation} : Density values, same length as `times`. ::: #### Details {.doc-details} Computed from the standardised density as $f_T(t) = f_\varepsilon(z) / (\sigma\,t)$, where $z = (\log t - \mu)/\sigma$. #### Examples {.doc-examples} Evaluate the Weibull density at a grid of time points: ::: {#41450419 .cell execution_count=4} ``` {.python .cell-code} import greenwood as gw lung = gw.load_dataset("lung", backend="polars") y = gw.Surv.right(lung["time"], event=(lung["status"] == 2)) fit = gw.Parametric("weibull").fit(y) fit.density([100, 200, 365, 500]) ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-display execution_count=4} ``` array([0.00172102, 0.00170849, 0.00130759, 0.00093992]) ``` ::: ::: ### [fit()]{.doc-object-name .doc-function .doc-label .doc-label-method} ::: {.doc-subject} Fit the distribution to right-censored survival data by maximum likelihood. ::: ::: {.doc-usage-source} Usage [Source](https://github.com/rich-iannone/greenwood/blob/main/greenwood/_univariate.py#L146-L225){target="_blank" rel="noopener"} ::: ::: {.doc-signature .doc-Kind.FUNCTION} ```python fit(surv) ``` ::: ::: {.doc-text} Maximises the likelihood of the parametric model $\log T = \mu + \sigma\varepsilon$ (intercept-only AFT) over the observed and censored times. The result is stored on the fitted object and reported in the natural parameterisation of each distribution. ::: #### Parameters {.doc-parameters} ::: {.doc-definition-items} [surv]{.doc-parameter-name}[:]{.doc-parameter-annotation-sep} [Surv]{.doc-parameter-annotation} : A right-censored `Surv` response built with `Surv.right()`. ::: #### Returns {.doc-returns} ::: {.doc-definition-items} [Parametric]{.doc-parameter-annotation} : The fitted object (for method chaining), with attributes `params_`, `std_error_`, `loglik_`, `aic_`, `bic_`, etc. ::: #### Examples {.doc-examples} Fit a log-normal distribution and view the estimated parameters with their standard errors: ::: {#660ecaf7 .cell execution_count=5} ``` {.python .cell-code} import greenwood as gw lung = gw.load_dataset("lung", backend="polars") y = gw.Surv.right(lung["time"], event=(lung["status"] == 2)) gw.Parametric("lognormal").fit(y) ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-display execution_count=5} ``` Parametric (lognormal distribution) estimate std_error mu 5.663 0.078 sigma 1.098 0.06187 n = 228, events = 165 Log-likelihood = -1169 AIC = 2343, BIC = 2349 ``` ::: ::: ### [hazard()]{.doc-object-name .doc-function .doc-label .doc-label-method} ::: {.doc-subject} Hazard function $h(t) = f(t) / S(t)$ at the given times. ::: ::: {.doc-usage-source} Usage [Source](https://github.com/rich-iannone/greenwood/blob/main/greenwood/_univariate.py#L364-L409){target="_blank" rel="noopener"} ::: ::: {.doc-signature .doc-Kind.FUNCTION} ```python hazard(times) ``` ::: ::: {.doc-text} The hazard (or hazard rate) gives the instantaneous risk of the event at time $t$, conditional on survival up to that time. Its shape reveals whether risk is increasing, decreasing, or constant over time. This shape is a signature feature of each distribution family. ::: #### Parameters {.doc-parameters} ::: {.doc-definition-items} [times]{.doc-parameter-name}[:]{.doc-parameter-annotation-sep} [Any]{.doc-parameter-annotation} : Query times (array-like of positive floats). ::: #### Returns {.doc-returns} ::: {.doc-definition-items} [ndarray]{.doc-parameter-annotation} : Hazard rate values, same length as `times`. ::: #### Details {.doc-details} The hazard is the ratio of the density to the survival function: $h(t) = f(t) / S(t)$. For a Weibull distribution, the hazard is monotone (increasing when shape > 1, decreasing when shape < 1, constant when shape = 1). For a log-logistic distribution, the hazard is non-monotone (rises then falls when shape > 1). #### Examples {.doc-examples} Evaluate the instantaneous hazard rate at several time points under a fitted Weibull model: ::: {#0936c2b7 .cell execution_count=6} ``` {.python .cell-code} import greenwood as gw lung = gw.load_dataset("lung", backend="polars") y = gw.Surv.right(lung["time"], event=(lung["status"] == 2)) fit = gw.Parametric("weibull").fit(y) fit.hazard([100, 200, 365, 500]) ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-display execution_count=6} ``` array([0.00200389, 0.00249604, 0.00302016, 0.00333684]) ``` ::: ::: ### [mean()]{.doc-object-name .doc-function .doc-label .doc-label-method} ::: {.doc-subject} Expected survival time $E[T]$. ::: ::: {.doc-usage-source} Usage [Source](https://github.com/rich-iannone/greenwood/blob/main/greenwood/_univariate.py#L494-L529){target="_blank" rel="noopener"} ::: ::: {.doc-signature .doc-Kind.FUNCTION} ```python mean() ``` ::: ::: {.doc-text} Returns the mean (expected) survival time under the fitted distribution. This is the area under the survival curve from zero to infinity. For skewed survival distributions, the mean can differ substantially from the median. ::: #### Returns {.doc-returns} ::: {.doc-definition-items} [float]{.doc-parameter-annotation} : The mean survival time under the fitted distribution. Returns `inf` for log-logistic when $\beta \le 1$ (the mean is undefined in that regime). ::: #### Details {.doc-details} Closed-form expressions are used for each family: - Weibull / Exponential: $E[T] = e^\mu\,\Gamma(1 + \sigma)$ - Log-normal: $E[T] = \exp(\mu + \sigma^2/2)$ - Log-logistic ($\beta > 1$): $E[T] = \alpha\pi/\beta\,/\,\sin(\pi/\beta)$ #### Examples {.doc-examples} Compute the mean survival time under a fitted Weibull model: ::: {#91caacac .cell execution_count=7} ``` {.python .cell-code} import greenwood as gw lung = gw.load_dataset("lung", backend="polars") y = gw.Surv.right(lung["time"], event=(lung["status"] == 2)) fit = gw.Parametric("weibull").fit(y) fit.mean() ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-display execution_count=7} ``` 384.85285283074927 ``` ::: ::: ### [median()]{.doc-object-name .doc-function .doc-label .doc-label-method} ::: {.doc-subject} Median survival time (the 50th-percentile time). ::: ::: {.doc-usage-source} Usage [Source](https://github.com/rich-iannone/greenwood/blob/main/greenwood/_univariate.py#L531-L556){target="_blank" rel="noopener"} ::: ::: {.doc-signature .doc-Kind.FUNCTION} ```python median() ``` ::: ::: {.doc-text} Returns the time at which half of subjects are expected to have experienced the event under the fitted distribution. The median is often preferred over the mean for reporting survival times because it is less sensitive to heavy tails. ::: #### Returns {.doc-returns} ::: {.doc-definition-items} [float]{.doc-parameter-annotation} : The time at which $S(t) = 0.5$. ::: #### Examples {.doc-examples} Compute the median survival time under a fitted Weibull model: ::: {#319d2bec .cell execution_count=8} ``` {.python .cell-code} import greenwood as gw lung = gw.load_dataset("lung", backend="polars") y = gw.Surv.right(lung["time"], event=(lung["status"] == 2)) fit = gw.Parametric("weibull").fit(y) fit.median() ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-display execution_count=8} ``` 316.26369237571606 ``` ::: ::: ### [quantile()]{.doc-object-name .doc-function .doc-label .doc-label-method} ::: {.doc-subject} Quantile function: survival-time quantiles at failure probabilities `p`. ::: ::: {.doc-usage-source} Usage [Source](https://github.com/rich-iannone/greenwood/blob/main/greenwood/_univariate.py#L454-L492){target="_blank" rel="noopener"} ::: ::: {.doc-signature .doc-Kind.FUNCTION} ```python quantile(p) ``` ::: ::: {.doc-text} The quantile $t_p$ satisfies $P(T \le t_p) = p$, so `p=0.5` gives the median survival time, `p=0.25` the first quartile, etc. This is the inverse of the CDF and provides clinically interpretable time-to-event summaries. ::: #### Parameters {.doc-parameters} ::: {.doc-definition-items} [p]{.doc-parameter-name}[:]{.doc-parameter-annotation-sep} [Any]{.doc-parameter-annotation} : Failure probabilities in `(0, 1)`. Scalar or array-like. ::: #### Returns {.doc-returns} ::: {.doc-definition-items} [ndarray]{.doc-parameter-annotation} : Survival times corresponding to each probability. ::: #### Details {.doc-details} The quantile is computed analytically by inverting the standardised error distribution: $t_p = \exp\!\bigl(\mu + \sigma\,F_\varepsilon^{-1}(p)\bigr)$. #### Examples {.doc-examples} Compute the first quartile, median, and third quartile of survival time under a fitted Weibull model: ::: {#f04d33ad .cell execution_count=9} ``` {.python .cell-code} import greenwood as gw lung = gw.load_dataset("lung", backend="polars") y = gw.Surv.right(lung["time"], event=(lung["status"] == 2)) fit = gw.Parametric("weibull").fit(y) fit.quantile([0.25, 0.5, 0.75]) ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-display execution_count=9} ``` array([162.19120953, 316.26369238, 535.36451702]) ``` ::: ::: ### [survival()]{.doc-object-name .doc-function .doc-label .doc-label-method} ::: {.doc-subject} Survival function $S(t) = P(T > t)$ at the given times. ::: ::: {.doc-usage-source} Usage [Source](https://github.com/rich-iannone/greenwood/blob/main/greenwood/_univariate.py#L284-L325){target="_blank" rel="noopener"} ::: ::: {.doc-signature .doc-Kind.FUNCTION} ```python survival(times) ``` ::: ::: {.doc-text} Returns the probability that a subject survives beyond each requested time under the fitted parametric distribution. Unlike the non-parametric Kaplan–Meier estimate, this gives a smooth curve that can be evaluated at any time point, including beyond the last observed event. ::: #### Parameters {.doc-parameters} ::: {.doc-definition-items} [times]{.doc-parameter-name}[:]{.doc-parameter-annotation-sep} [Any]{.doc-parameter-annotation} : Query times (array-like of positive floats). ::: #### Returns {.doc-returns} ::: {.doc-definition-items} [ndarray]{.doc-parameter-annotation} : Survival probabilities, same length as `times`. ::: #### Details {.doc-details} The survival probability is computed from the fitted location–scale model as $S(t) = 1 - F_\varepsilon\!\bigl((\log t - \mu)/\sigma\bigr)$, where $F_\varepsilon$ is the CDF of the standardised error distribution. #### Examples {.doc-examples} Evaluate the fitted Weibull survival function at a few clinically relevant time points (100, 200, 365, and 500 days): ::: {#dce91382 .cell execution_count=10} ``` {.python .cell-code} import greenwood as gw lung = gw.load_dataset("lung", backend="polars") y = gw.Surv.right(lung["time"], event=(lung["status"] == 2)) fit = gw.Parametric("weibull").fit(y) fit.survival([100, 200, 365, 500]) ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-display execution_count=10} ``` array([0.85883885, 0.68448015, 0.43295354, 0.28167901]) ``` ::: ::: ### [to_frame()]{.doc-object-name .doc-function .doc-label .doc-label-method} ::: {.doc-subject} Return the parameter estimates as a tidy DataFrame. ::: ::: {.doc-usage-source} Usage [Source](https://github.com/rich-iannone/greenwood/blob/main/greenwood/_univariate.py#L560-L600){target="_blank" rel="noopener"} ::: ::: {.doc-signature .doc-Kind.FUNCTION} ```python to_frame(*, format=None) ``` ::: ::: {.doc-text} One row per parameter, with columns `param`, `estimate`, `std_error`, `conf_low`, and `conf_high`. ::: #### Parameters {.doc-parameters} ::: {.doc-definition-items} [format]{.doc-parameter-name}[:]{.doc-parameter-annotation-sep} [str | None]{.doc-parameter-annotation} [=]{.doc-parameter-default-sep .op} [None]{.doc-parameter-default} : Output format: `None` (auto-detect), `"pandas"`, `"polars"`, or `"pyarrow"`. ::: #### Returns {.doc-returns} ::: {.doc-definition-items} [DataFrame]{.doc-parameter-annotation} : A tidy parameter table. ::: #### Examples {.doc-examples} Export the parameter estimates as a Polars DataFrame for further analysis or reporting: ::: {#509f68f8 .cell execution_count=11} ``` {.python .cell-code} import greenwood as gw lung = gw.load_dataset("lung", backend="polars") y = gw.Surv.right(lung["time"], event=(lung["status"] == 2)) fit = gw.Parametric("weibull").fit(y) fit.to_frame(format="polars") ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-display execution_count=11} ```{=html}
shape: (2, 5)

strf64f64f64f64
"shape"1.316840.082211.1557121.477969
"scale"417.75865924.704538369.338654466.178663
``` ::: :::
